explanation: The natural language states a conditional: if things are not going according to plan (that is, if 'unexpectedCondition' holds), then an action is required (notify users). This is a global property, meaning it should be true at all times, so we use the G operator. The situation is formalized as: G(unexpectedCondition -> notifyUsers).

explanation_dict.natural_language: things are not proceeding according to plan (unexpected event or situation has occurred)
notify users that something unexpected has happened
if unexpected condition occurs, then notify users
always (globally) apply this condition

explanation: The natural language expresses two cases: (1) If things are proceeding according to plan (i.e., no unexpected condition), then little action is needed; (2) if things are NOT proceeding according to plan (i.e., an unexpected condition occurs), then you need to respond (notify users). This is most naturally modeled as: whenever 'unexpectedCondition' occurs, eventually 'notifyUsers' will happen. This is formalized as G(unexpectedCondition -> F notifyUsers).

explanation_dict.natural_language: You want to respond whenever an unexpected event occurs.
This response should always hold throughout execution.

explanation: The specification states: 'If things are proceeding according to plan, you need very little directional information. If they're not, then you want to dig in and get back into micromanaging.' Translating this into LTL with the atomic propositions: 'unexpectedCondition' represents things not proceeding according to plan, and 'notifyUsers' represents the action when things are not according to plan (i.e., micromanaging or notification). Thus, whenever an unexpectedCondition happens, the response (notifyUsers) should eventually occur. This is expressed as: G(unexpectedCondition -> F notifyUsers), meaning that globally, whenever unexpectedCondition is true, then notifyUsers will eventually be true.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Unexpected event or deviation (not according to plan) occurs
Notify users (micromanaging action) occurs
if not according to plan, then micromanage/notify
always apply the rule

explanation: The requirement describes two cases: (1) When things are proceeding according to plan, little information is needed (no extra action). (2) If things are not going according to plan (i.e., some unexpected condition holds), you want to 'dig in' and actively manage (here, represented as notify users). We interpret this as: Whenever an unexpected condition occurs, eventually there should be a notification to users. This is usually written as 'Globally, if unexpectedCondition holds, then eventually notifyUsers', or: G(unexpectedCondition -> F notifyUsers).

notify users that something unexpected has happened
if unexpected event or situation has occurred, eventually notify users
globally

explanation: The requirement describes two modes: One, when things proceed as planned (unexpectedCondition is false = !unexpectedCondition), and in this case, no special action (like notification) is needed. Two, when things are 'not' proceeding according to plan (unexpectedCondition holds), there is a need for 'digging in,' which can be represented as notifyUsers should happen. The conditional - if unexpectedCondition, then notifyUsers - needs to be enforced globally. Since the 'digging in' or notification should be done every time an unexpected situation arises, the formalization is G(unexpectedCondition -> notifyUsers).

they're not proceeding according to plan
If they're not, then you want to dig in (notify)
If things are proceeding according to plan, you need very little directional information (no notify)

explanation: To translate this requirement, we focus on the reactive property: When things are not proceeding according to plan (i.e., an unexpected condition occurs), you should respond, which here we map to 'notify users.' This is a typical response property and translates to 'Globally, if unexpectedCondition then eventually notifyUsers,' or G(unexpectedCondition -> F notifyUsers). This means that whenever an unexpected situation arises, eventually the system will notify users.

explanation: The sentence describes two cases: (1) When things are proceeding according to plan (i.e., no unexpected condition), little information/control is needed. (2) When things are NOT proceeding according to plan (i.e., unexpected condition occurs), then a response (dig in/micromanage, here mapped to 'notify users') should happen. We formalize only the latter, as it specifies a reactive requirement: If an unexpected condition occurs, then users must eventually be notified. In LTL, 'if unexpectedCondition then eventually notifyUsers' is represented as (unexpectedCondition -> F notifyUsers), and the global scope 'whenever this happens' is captured with the G (globally) operator, leading to G(unexpectedCondition -> F notifyUsers).

explanation: The natural language sentence can be captured by representing two scenarios: (1) if everything is proceeding as planned, minimal action is needed; (2) if an unexpected condition occurs, action is triggered (here: notify users). This can be encoded as: 'Globally, if an unexpected condition occurs, then eventually notify users.' This covers the reactive part: notification action is required only when something unexpected happens. The formula is G(unexpectedCondition -> F notifyUsers).
